paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — Bedok View Secondary School Preliminary 2021 SA2 Paper 2 §B

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about an African young boy who encountered a dying deer in the veld while he was out hunting, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [20 marks] from Bedok View Secondary School Preliminary Examination 2021 Paper 1128/02. The original Section B carries 11 questions worth 20 marks; this fixture ingests the 9 short-answer items that have a single objective canonical answer plus 4 MCQ items derived from Q16's emotion-flowchart task. The 3-mark literary-analysis Q6, the simile-table Q7, and the literary-device Q11 are omitted as they require open-ended marker judgement.
sections.passageTitle: The young hunter's encounter with a dying deer (The Sunrise on the Veld by Doris Lessing)
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
There it lay, a chocolate-horned figure with legs splayed out. From afar, it looked like a young deer with black ragged tufts of hair sprouting irregularly all over its limp body with patches of raw flesh. But the patches of rawness were disappearing under moving black patches and reappearing elsewhere. 'How odd,' he thought. He ran closer to the wailing creature as it heaved out small gasps in between and writhed drunkenly from side to side.

Paragraph 2
Upon getting closer, then he knew. He saw the ground — black with ants, great energetic ants that took no notice of him; like a speed train bulleting towards the fighting shape, like glistening black water flowing through the grass. As he drew in his breath while terror and pity seized him, the deer finally gave in under the twisting blackness and the wailing stopped. Now he could hear nothing but the sound of the rustling whispering ants.

Paragraph 3
He raised his rifle to end its pain, but lowered it back. It can no longer feel; its convulsions are a mechanical protest of the nerves. But that was not what made him put down his weapon. It was a swelling feeling of rage, misery and protest that expressed itself in the thought, 'If I had not come, it would have died like this regardless. So, why should I interfere? All over the bush, things like this happen, they happen all the time. This is how life goes on; by living things dying in anguish.' As though on cue, he suddenly felt in his limbs the myriad swarming pain of the twitching animal that could no longer feel. With gritted teeth, he repeated under his breath: 'I couldn't have prevented it. There is nothing I can do now.'

Paragraph 4
Suffering, sick, and angry, but also grimly satisfied with his new stoicism, he stood there leaning on his rifle, and watched the seething black mound grow smaller. He followed the ants with his eyes and saw them manoeuvring around his feet to trickle back into the grass with pink fragments in their mouths. Seeing those pink bits, he turned away and sternly controlled the convulsing muscles of his stomach, and to his surprise, found hot tears streaming down his cheeks.

Paragraph 5
The helpless shape had grown small. Now it looked unrecognisable. He did not know how long it was before he saw the blackness thin, and bits of white showed through. He strode forward, crushing ants with each step, till he stood above the skeleton, which lay sprawled. It was clean-picked. Ants were ebbing away from the bones, their pincers full of meat. The boy looked at them, big black ugly insects. A few were standing and gazing up at him with small glittering eyes. 'Go away!' he ordered the ants, very coldly. And he fancied that the ants turned and went away.

Paragraph 6
He bent over the bones and touched the sockets in the skull; that was where the eyes had been. He thought incredulously. What had happened? The boy bent curiously towards the skeletal frame. Then he saw that the back leg that lay uppermost strained out in the tension of death, was snapped midway in the thigh, so that broken bones jutted over each other uselessly. So that was it! Limping into the ant-masses which it could not escape from, once it had sensed the danger. Yes, but how had the leg been broken? Had it fallen, perhaps? Impossible, a deer was too light and graceful. What could possibly have happened?

Paragraph 7
Even as he imagined the crowd of running and shouting hunters, and the flying stones, and the leaping deer, another picture came into his mind. He saw himself: on any one of these bright mornings while hunting, discovering a dying deer. He saw himself with the rifle lowered, wondering whether he should end its torture. Then, as suddenly as the thought came, it suddenly went away. He realised that it was getting late in the morning and that he desired for his breakfast, and he figured that it was not worthwhile to reflect on his actions for an animal that would have died anyway.

Paragraph 8
For a moment, he would not face it. He was a small boy again, kicking sulkily at the skeleton, hanging his head, refusing to accept the responsibility. Then he straightened up, and looked down at the bones with an odd expression of dismay, all the anger gone out of him. His mind went quite empty: all around him he could see trickles of ants disappearing into the grass. The whispering noise was faint and dry, like the rustling of a cast snakeskin.

(Adapted from The Sunrise on the Veld by Doris Lessing. veld: flat open land with grass and no trees in South Africa.)

questions.stem: Q8. What is effective about the phrase 'twisting blackness' (line 9, Paragraph 2)? [1]
questions.answer: it shows the large number of ants attacking the dying deer

questions.stem: Q9(a). In Paragraph 3, why did the boy grit his teeth? [1]
questions.answer: he was trying to control his overwhelming emotions

questions.stem: Q9(b). Which word in Paragraph 4 conveys the same idea as in Q9(a)? [1]
Q10. In lines 23–24, 'to his surprise, found hot tears streaming down his cheeks'. Why was the young boy surprised? [1]
questions.answer: he had already come to terms with the deer's death so he did not expect to cry over it

questions.stem: Q12. Explain what was happening when the boy 'fancied that the ants turned and went away' (lines 30-31). [2]
questions.answer: he imagined the ants were obeying his order, but they were actually just moving away on their own

questions.stem: Q13. In Paragraph 6, what was the realisation the boy had when he thought, 'So that was it!' (line 36)? [1]
questions.answer: the boy discovered how the deer died

questions.stem: Q14(a). In Paragraph 7, give one quotation that explains why the boy was not bothered by his guilt. [1]
Q14(b). In Paragraph 7, give a second quotation that explains why the boy was not bothered by his guilt. [1]
questions.answer: it was not worthwhile to reflect on his actions for an animal that would have died anyway

questions.stem: Q15. In Paragraph 8, 'For a moment, he would not face it. He was a small boy again, kicking sulkily at the skeleton, hanging his head, refusing to accept the responsibility.' (lines 46-47). What do the phrases 'he would not face it' and 'refusing to accept the responsibility' suggest about the attitude of the boy towards the death of the deer? [1]
questions.answer: he was in denial of his responsibility for the deer's death

sections.instruction: Q16. The structure of the text reflects the boy's thoughts and feelings as he witnessed the dying deer being attacked and eaten slowly by the ants. Complete the flowchart by choosing one word from the box below to summarise the main thoughts or feelings presented in each part of the text. There are some extra words you do not need to use. Word bank: disgusted / determined / doubtful / nonchalant / amazed / conflicted / puzzled. The original task (Q16, [4]) asks students to fill four blanks for Paragraph 1, Paragraph 3, Paragraphs 4-5, and Paragraphs 7-8 (Paragraph 2 'terrified' and Paragraph 6 'inquisitive' are given). Each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same Sunrise on the Veld narrative in Exercise 1 above. The boy's dominant feeling shifts as the scene unfolds — from initial confusion at the strange-looking creature, through inner conflict over whether to interfere, then revulsion at the ants eating the deer, ending with resolute justification of his inaction. Pick the single best word from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q16(i). What is the dominant feeling in Paragraph 1 (the boy first encountering the strange-looking, writhing 'creature' from afar before realising it was a deer being eaten by ants)? [1]
Q16(ii). What is the dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 (the boy raised his rifle to end the deer's pain but then lowered it back, debating whether he should interfere)? [1]

Q16(iii). What is the dominant feeling in Paragraphs 4-5 (the boy seeing the ants carrying pink bits of flesh, finding hot tears streaming down, then crushing ants underfoot and coldly ordering them to go away)? [1]

Q16(iv). What is the dominant feeling in Paragraphs 7-8 (the boy resolving that it was not worthwhile to reflect on his actions for an animal that would have died anyway, refusing to accept responsibility)? [1]
